Construction Period: 1632 AD - 1653 AD (Main mausoleum completed 1648 AD)
UNESCO Criteria: (i) to represent a masterpiece of human creative genius;
Local Stone Name: Makrana Marble (Sang-e-Marmar / Pure White Calcite Marble)
Lithology: Metamorphic (Granoblastic Calcitic Marble with ~98–100% Calcite); Secondary stone is Sedimentary Quartzarenite Sandstone
Formation: Ajmer Formation, Kumbhalgarh Group, Delhi Supergroup
Texture: Fine to medium-grained, crystalline granoblastic, interlocking texture
Minerals: Calcite (>98%), with minor quartz, muscovite, and silicate impurities in grey streaks
Quarry: Makrana (Nagaur District, Rajasthan) for marble; Tantpur/Dholpur for red sandstone
Local vs Imported: Imported (Transported from Nagaur, Rajasthan to Agra, Uttar Pradesh)
Transport Distance: ~300 km to 350 km (from Makrana, Rajasthan to Agra)
Structural Use: Main tomb structure, minarets, platforms, domes, and wall veneering.
Decorative Use: Exquisite octagonal marble lattice screens, pietra dura stone intarsia inlay (using precious/semi-precious stones), floral relief motifs, and Arabic calligraphic inscriptions.
Masonry Technique: Brick-in-lime mortar core veneered with dressed stone slices.
Weathering: Atmospheric pollution-induced discolouration; micro-cracking and bio-deterioration
Restoration: Traditional clay pack treatments (Fuller's earth / multani mitti) for cleaning marble yellowing; air quality monitoring; banning coal/coke usage in the 10,400 $\text{km}^2$ Taj Trapezium Zone (TTZ)

Architecture Type: Dravidian Architecture (Temples, Gopurams, Mandapas) and Indo-Islamic Architecture (Secular buildings like Queen's Bath and Elephant Stables).
Construction Period: 14th to 16th Century CE (Vijayanagara Empire, 1336–1565 CE; building activity spanning over 200 years).
Civilization: Vijayanagara Empire (The last great Hindu Kingdom of South India).
Local vs Imported: Local ("constructed from local granite", "utilizing locally available material")
Structural Use: Fortification walls, temple complexes, pillared halls (Mandapas), shrines, entrance towers (Gopurams), ceremonial pavilions, heavy roof slabs, stone chariot (granite ratha), stepped tanks (Pushkarani), bridges, and water channels.
Decorative Use: Intricately carved stone chariot (Vittala Temple), ornate decorated pillars, relief sculptures, carved entrance gopurams, and sculpted lintels/capitals.
Masonry Technique: Post-and-lintel system, lantern-roofed construction, dry-stone masonry with paper joints (rubble core for fortification walls), brick-and-lime mortar superstructures (for Gopuras), and heavy granite roof slabs sealed with lime mortar waterproofing.
Restoration: Ongoing conservation and preservation by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and Department of Archaeology and Museums (DAM), guided by the Integrated Management Plan (IMP) and Hampi World Heritage Area Management Authority (HWHAMA).
Other references: Official ICOMOS Evaluation & UNESCO Dossier Documents

Site Name: Sudanese style mosques in northern Côte d’Ivoire
Construction Period: Originated 12th–14th centuries (Djenné); spread 15th century; surviving mosques built mainly between the 17th and 19th centuries
Civilization: Islamic / Mandinka (Mande) and local indigenous Gur communities
Lithology: Clay, silt, organic temper, and localized lateritic earth
Colour: Earth tones (brownish-yellow, tan, reddish-brown earthen plaster)
Texture: Smooth to coarse earthen plaster finish
Local vs Imported: Local
Structural Use: Load-bearing walls, vertical buttresses, massive pilasters, truncated pyramid minarets, qibla towers, roof support
Decorative Use: Projecting wooden poles (torons), pottery or ostrich egg finials topping buttresses and minarets, mitres crowning roof
Masonry Technique: Cob (bousillage) and adobe (earthen brick) masonry layered with earthen plaster, reinforced with timber scaffolding (torons)
Weathering: Erosion and degradation caused by humid climatic conditions, intense rain, urbanization, and population growth
Replacement Stone: Earth, timber, and traditional clay plasters (Reversible modern materials used in recent interventions need replacement with traditional adobe/cob)
Restoration: Regular community-led maintenance, re-plastering by traditional masons, local grassroots management committees
Other references: Advisory Body Evaluation (ICOMOS), Decree No. 2020-121, Law No. 87-806, Law No. 98-750

Site Name: Fort and Shalamar Gardens in Lahore
Architecture Type: Fortified Palace & Water Garden Enclosure Complex

Colour: Red/Terracotta (Sandstone); Pure White, Milky White, Grey/Pink Streaked (Marble)
Structural Use: Baked brick masonry core with red sandstone courses, enclosing walls, gateways, bastions, and supporting pillars/pavilions
Decorative Use: Luxurious marble palaces, screens, inlay of semi-precious stones (pietra dura), glass mosaics (Shish Mahal), gilt, and zoomorphic corbels
Masonry Technique: Ashlar stone cladding over baked brick, carved stone lattice (jaali) screens, corbelled brackets, and stone inlay/pietra dura

Restoration: Traditional lime mortar and matching red sandstone/marble matching historical composition during controlled restoration

Architecture Type: Multifunctional structure of the khanaqa type (combines a mausoleum and a mosque)
Construction Period: 1389–1405 (Timurid period)
Civilization: Timurid Empire / Central Asian Islamic Civilization
Minerals: Gypsum, Carbonates, nitrates, sulfates, chlorides (in salt efflorescence)
Local vs Imported: Local clay for fired bricks; Persian master craft influence for decorative techniques
Structural Use: Vaults, domes (conic-spherical dome), walls, arches, drums
Decorative Use: Alabaster stalactites (muqarnas) in dome intrados, glazed tile facing, inscriptions (Kufic and Suls)

Country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
Architecture Type: Megalithic stone circles, henges, barrows, avenues, and earthen earthworks/mounds.
Construction Period: Circa 3700 BC – 1600 BC (Neolithic to Early Bronze Age); focal stone circle built ~2500 BC.
Civilization: Prehistoric Britain (Neolithic and Bronze Age societies).
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Wiltshire Sarsen Sandstone and Bluestone (dolerite, rhyolite, tuffs)
Secondary Stone: Chalk (used in earthworks/mounds like Silbury Hill), Altar Stone (Metasandstone), Quartz veins, packing stones
Local vs Imported:
Sarsens: Local/Regional (~25 km).
Bluestones: Imported (~200–240 km).
Local vs Imported_Ext: Nash, D. J., Ciborowski, T. J. R., Ullyott, J. S., Pearson, M. P., Darvill, T., Greaney, S., Maniatis, G., & Whitaker, K. A. (2020). Origins of the sarsen megaliths at Stonehenge. Science Advances, 6(31), eabc0133. https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abc0133
Transport Distance: ~25 km for Sarsens; up to 240 km for Bluestones.
Structural Use: Standing megalithic uprights, horizontal lintels, central trilithons, inner/outer concentric circles, horseshoe arrangements, packing stones.
Decorative Use: Unique surface shaping/dressing of megalithic stones; intentional arrangement of bluestone contrast.
Masonry Technique: Mortise-and-tenon jointing, tongue-and-groove lintel capping, dressed/shaped megaliths.
Weathering: Weathering from surface erosion, natural environmental exposure, animal burrowing, and historic visitor traffic.
Restoration: Early to mid-20th-century interventions involving excavation, re-erection of fallen or buried stones to their original positions, and modern site consolidation.
Other references:
1. Nash et al., 2020 - Science Advances


2. Parker Pearson et al., 2015 - Antiquity


3. Whitaker, 2019 - Oxford Journal of Archaeology


4. Lott, 2013 - Quarterly Journal of Engineering Geology and Hydrogeology

Quarry Country: Local coastal coral reefs and nearby river quarries (e.g., Meycauayan/Guadalupe tuff quarries)

Architecture Type: Buddhist Religious Architectural Complex / Artificial Stone Grotto & Wooden Temple on Stone Terraces
Construction Period: 8th Century (Built circa 751–774 CE)
Texture: Equigranular to porphyritic masonry blocks and reliefs
Quarry: Mount Toham (Mount Tohamsan) quarries
Local vs Imported: Local (sourced directly from Mount Tohamsan)
Transport Distance: Short distance / Local site extraction (< 5 km)
Structural Use: Artificial domed grotto, stone foundations, raised terraces, steps, and pagodas (Seokgatap and Dabotap)

Site Name: Temple and Cemetery of Confucius and the Kong Family Mansion in Qufu

Architecture Type: Traditional Earthen / Islamic Sub-Saharan (Sudano-Sahelian style featuring verticality and buttresses)
Construction Period: Inhabited since 250 B.C.; current architectural fabric dates from 15th–16th centuries (with Moroccan influences in 1591, Toucouleur in 1862, and Mosque reconstruction in 1906–1907)
Civilization: Pre-Islamic / Islamic West African (Trans-Saharan trade period)
Lithology: Silt, clay, and alluvial sand (for sun-dried mud bricks / djénné ferey)
Quarry: Local floodplains (toguere) and marshes along the Bani/Niger Rivers
Local vs Imported: Local
Transport Distance: Very Short (< 5 km from surrounding floodplains)
Structural Use: Load-bearing mud brick walls, monumental pilasters, and vertical buttresses
Decorative Use: Façade relief motifs (potige), ancestral entrance archways, decorative pilasters
Masonry Technique: Djenné Ferey (traditional cylindrical or rectangular sun-dried mud bricks laid with earthen mortar and plaster)
Weathering: Leaching, rainwater erosion, gullying from severe seasonal rains and floods
Replacement Stone: Earthen plaster re-application (Crépissage), cement/fired bricks (noted as an aesthetic threat/modification)
Restoration: Annual community plastering ritual (Crépissage de la Grande Mosquée); traditional masonry maintenance by the Barey Guild

Site Name: Villages with Fortified Churches in Transylvania
Architecture Type: Architectural, Building, Modern Movement (Fortified Churches, Castles, Towers, Palaces)
Construction Period: Late Middle Ages / 13th to 16th century
4th millennium BC and 3rd millennium BC
Lithology: Hard coralline limestone, Soft globigerina limestone
Local vs Imported: Locally available stone
Structural Use: External walls, sheltered interiors, orthostats (upright stone slabs), horizontal blocks, paved courts, corbelled roofs, horizontal beams
Decorative Use: Decorated features, panels decorated with drilled holes, bas-relief panels depicting spiral motifs, trees, plants, and animals
Masonry Technique: Megalithic masonry; upright stone slabs (orthostats) surmounted by horizontal blocks; corbelled roofing; external walls constructed in larger blocks set alternately face out and edge out with space between inner/outer walls filled with stones and earth
Weathering: Material and structural deterioration, erosion by natural and human causes, weathering
Replacement Stone: Cement (used for capping surviving blocks during historic restoration)
Restoration: Moving decorated blocks indoors; capping surviving blocks with cement; lightweight, removable protective shelters/covers implemented

Site Name: Walled City of Baku with the Shirvanshah's Palace and Maiden Tower
White Monuments of Vladimir and Suzdal

Site Name: Historic Jeddah, the Gate to Makkah
Architecture Type: Multi-cultural Red Sea architectural tradition, characterized by imposing Roshan tower houses and lower houses, mosques, ribat-s, suqs, and small public squares.
Construction Period: 7th century AD / 7th century AH (established as port/gate), with major development of tower houses occurring in the late 19th century (especially post-1869 after the Suez Canal opening).
Civilization: Islamic / Arabian Red Sea cultural world (influenced by Indian Ocean trade routes and a cosmopolitan population of pilgrims and merchants from Asia, Africa, and the Middle East).
Local vs Imported: Local (Red Sea coastal coral building traditions)
Structural Use: Coral stone/coral masonry used for constructing lower houses and tower houses (combined with embedded wooden beams).
Decorative Use: Precious woodwork / Roshan wooden screens decorating facades (no stone decorative details mentioned).
Masonry Technique: Coral masonry construction with embedded wooden beams; isolated, outward-oriented houses with specific technical devices for internal ventilation.
Weathering: Decay and deterioration of historic structures due to humidity, heat, abandonment, and squatting by poor immigrants.
Restoration: Punctual restoration, stabilization, and revitalization projects aimed at nursing buildings back to life; minor maintenance on original masonries.

Site Name: Episcopal Complex of the Euphrasian Basilica in the Historic Centre of Poreč

Granite: Columns and beams


Sand: Sandbox foundation


Porous Clay Bricks: Lightweight pyramidal Vimana (horizontally stepped tower)

Architecture Type: Traditional vernacular dry-stone dwellings/huts (trulli) with corbelled conical roofs; historic urban landscape

Site Name: Assisi, the Basilica of San Francesco and Other Franciscan Sites
Architecture Type: Umbrian hill town, medieval city, basilicas, sanctuaries, fort, hermitage sites
Construction Period: Ancient Roman origins, 13th century (medieval) through late 15th–18th centuries
Quarry: Mount Subasio slopes (local hill context)
Local vs Imported: Local
Structural Use: Walls, foundations, vaults, basilicas, fort (Rocca Maggiore), sanctuaries
Masonry Technique: Stone construction / terraced Roman town foundation
Restoration: Restoration works carried out following earthquake damage (e.g. collapse of parts of the vaults in the Basilica of San Francesco)

Construction Period: Sinhala kings period until 1815
14th–16th Century (Apogee under Amir Temur & Temurids); Ak-Sarai Palace construction began 1380
Structural Use: Gate foundations, Mausoleum/Tomb structures, City Walls, Cupola
Decorative Use: Façade facing (white marble), rich palace decoration
Masonry Technique: Traditional materials and techniques
Weathering: Inroads of time; rising ground water level affecting physical integrity
Restoration: Care taken to ensure use of traditional materials and techniques

Architecture Type: Renaissance, Mannerism, Neoclassicism (18th century addition), Monumental, Tower
Construction Period: Turn of the 16th and 17th centuries (1584), remodelled/heightened late 18th century (1788–1789)
Structural Use: Monumental tower, frustoconical tower, pilasters, columns, dome, light chamber, 8 levels (67m high)
Decorative Use: Pilasters, columns, sculptures, modillions, gargoyles, king's apartments, chapel
Masonry Technique: Dressed blocks, stereotomy / stereometry
Weathering: Highly exposed and hostile/aggressive maritime environment (extreme meteorological conditions, hazards, ocean exposure)
Restoration: Heightened/strengthened in 1788–1789; regular technical adaptations and restorations in 19th & 20th centuries (addition of annular buildings)

Architecture Type: Hispano-American Baroque, Art Nouveau, Colonial, Republican, Traditional civil & religious architecture
Construction Period: 16th Century to 20th Century A.D. (Viceroyalty to Modern period)
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Adobe, Stone, Brick, Quincha (traditional mud & organic lattice)
Local vs Imported: Local
Structural Use: First-floor walls (Adobe & Brick), Bridge construction (Stone over Rímac River), Foundations
Decorative Use: Facades, Hallways, Patios, Closed ("box") Balconies
Masonry Technique: Hybrid Anti-Seismic Masonry: Adobe and brick on the ground floor, Quincha on upper floors
Weathering: Environmental pollution, vibrations from transportation, natural material degradation
Restoration: Stabilization post-earthquakes (1586, 1687, 1746, 1940, 1966, 1970, 1974); Ongoing municipal plans (EMILIMA)

Architecture Type: Monastery, Pilgrimage Centre, Ecclesiastical Complex (Churches, Baptistery, Basilicas, Hostels/Rest Houses, Public Baths, Workshops)
Construction Period: 4th to 7th centuries AD (A.D. 296 – 7th Century AD)
Structural Use: Ashlar masonry for main buildings / wall construction
Decorative Use: Columns (made of Marble), Mosaics, fine lime plaster
Masonry Technique: Ashlar masonry set in lime mortar; Mud bricks covered with fine lime plaster for simpler buildings
Weathering: Risks from heavy rains, winds, humidity, fires, and rising underground water related to agricultural irrigation
Restoration: Ongoing periodic maintenance and active dewatering project to lower underground water table

Site Name: Buddhist Monuments in the Horyu-ji Area
Construction Period: Late 7th century or early 8th century (Rebuilding commenced after 670 fire)
Civilization: Japanese / Adaptation of Chinese Buddhist culture via the Korean peninsula

Construction Period: 12th to 13th centuries (1117 CE onwards)
Structural Use: Stellate sanctum, circumambulatory platform, adhisthana, walls, temples
Decorative Use: Sculptural gallery, multi-tiered friezes, hyper-real sculptures, stone carvings, Sala legend sculptures
Masonry Technique: Stellate plan, horizontal friezes, fine stone carving
Restoration: Past alterations noted (loss of superstructures, loss of enclosure walls)

Architecture Type: Baroque theatre architecture / Court opera house
Civilization: 18th-century European / Princely Court (Margravine Wilhelmine of Brandenburg-Bayreuth)
Structural Use: Sandstone façade (designed by Joseph Saint Pierre)
Restoration: Overall conservation and restoration plan approved by State Party; restoration program concluded

Architecture Type: Classical Greek / Sanctuary & Monumental Complex (Parthenon, Erechtheion, Propylaea, Temple of Athena Nike)
Construction Period: 5th Century BC (with earliest fortifications dating to 13th Century BC)
Quarry: Mt. Penteli (Pentelikon)
Local vs Imported: Pentelic Marble / Penteli Marble
Structural Use: Temples, Propylaea, Fortification Walls, Foundations, Altars
Decorative Use: Sculptural and Architectural elements
Masonry Technique: Ancient classical masonry/building techniques
Weathering: Eroded architectural elements
Replacement Stone: White marble quarried from Mt. Penteli
Restoration: Integrated intervention program ongoing since 1975 following the principles of the Venice Charter

Architecture Type: Hellenistic orthogonal plan, Romanesque churches, Gothic and Renaissance buildings/palaces, Baroque, Byzantine, Venetian fortifications, Fortress (Camerlengo), Cathedral, Basilica, Town Hall
Construction Period: Hellenistic period (3rd century BC), Medieval period, 13th to 16th century (Cathedral built ~1200–late 16th century), 14th–15th century (Town Hall), Venetian period
Civilization: Greek colonists, Roman, Byzantine, Hungarian, Venetian
Local vs Imported: Local
Structural Use: Walls, residential blocks, fortifications, cathedral, basilica, bell tower, foundations, town hall, palaces
Decorative Use: Sculpture, portals (south portal, main west portal by Master Radovan)
Masonry Technique: Megalithic wall, Vaulting (main nave)
Weathering: Sea level rise, climate change, past heavy vehicle traffic, patina accumulation
Replacement Stone: Authentic materials (where replacement is necessary)
Restoration: Rejection of abrasive stone cleaning in favor of maintaining patina; authentic materials and traditional techniques employed

Architecture Type: Architectural, Architecture, Building, Byzantine, Design, Facade, Monument, Monumental, Plan, Structure, Traditional (also Nabataean, Hellenistic, Roman, Graeco-Roman)
Construction Period: Prehistoric times; first centuries BC to AD (Nabataean, Roman, Byzantine); Iron Age, Chalcolithic, Crusader/Medieval
Civilization: Nabataean, Roman, Byzantine, Hellenistic, Assyrian, Greek, Crusader
Local vs Imported: Local (rock-cut directly into surrounding cliffs and gorges)
Structural Use: Half-built, half-carved rock-cut structures, tombs, temples, water channels, dams, cisterns, city walls, arches
Decorative Use: Monumental rock-cut facades, capitals, funerary stelae
Masonry Technique: Rock-cut / Ashlar Freestanding Masonry
Weathering: Ongoing erosion due to wind and rain, exacerbated by windblown sand and natural decay of the sandstone architecture
Restoration: Stabilization of freestanding monuments including Qasr al Bint temple and the vaulted structure supporting the Byzantine forecourt

Site Name: Cathedral of Notre-Dame, Former Abbey of Saint-Rémi and Palace of Tau, Reims
Historic Town of St George and Related Fortifications, Bermuda
Country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
Architecture Type: Colonial, English military engineering, Garrison town
Construction Period: 17th to 20th century (Settlement began August 1612)
Quarry Country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland (Bermuda)
Local vs Imported: Local materials / Local stone
Structural Use: Walls, roofs, foundations, fortification batteries, seawalls
Masonry Technique: Early masonry construction / Cut soft stone blocks whitewashed for water catchment
Weathering: Storm damage, exposure to maritime environments, invasive plant species
Restoration: Major restorations (Seawalls, roof, window restorations, structural repairs, graffiti removal)

Site Name: Aqueduct of Padre Tembleque Hydraulic System
Architecture Type: Hydraulic system, arcaded aqueduct bridges, canals, distribution tanks, cisterns/reservoirs, springs
Construction Period: 1555–1572 (16th Century)
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Adobe (earthen adobe brick formwork), Stone (enclosed in stone)
Local vs Imported: Local ( adaptation of local methods of adobe construction and regional materials)
Structural Use: Supporting structures / formwork using earthen adobes; arcaded aqueduct bridges; main and secondary canals
Decorative Use: Glyphs illustrating symbols and cosmology incorporated in arcade structures
Masonry Technique: Roman masonry aqueduct tradition combined with Mesoamerican adobe construction and collective labor techniques
Restoration: Ongoing repairs and maintenance undertaken by local communities using traditional construction techniques and materials

Site Name: Royal Exhibition Building and Carlton Gardens
Architecture Type: Byzantine, Romanesque, Lombardic and Italian Renaissance
Construction Period: Designed for international exhibitions of 1880 and 1888 (constructed starting 1879)
Structural Use: Plinth, base of iron railings, slate roof, timber floors, external joinery and stonework
Decorative Use: Internal paintings/murals, parapet ornamentation
Masonry Technique: Brick and timber, steel and slate construction
Replacement Stone: Concrete (replaced original timber staircases early in the 20th century)
Restoration: Dome, structure, external joinery and stonework, timber floors, scroll/parterre gardens, and reinstatement of missing parapet ornamentation

Architecture Type: Muslim Military Architecture (Fortress / Garrison complex with bastions, monumental gateways, inner citadel, stepped wells, mosque, and haveli)
Construction Period: 16th Century (Commenced in 1541 by Sher Shah Suri; continuous use until 1707 and reoccupied into the 18th/19th centuries)
Civilization: Suri Dynasty / Mughal Empire (incorporating artistic and architectural traditions from Turkey and the Indian Subcontinent)
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Marble and Sandstone (Rock/geological material is explicitly mentioned)
Structural Use: Massive defensive fortification walls, 68 bastions, 12 monumental gateways, inner citadel walls, stepped wells (baolis), and buildings (Shahi Masjid, Haveli Man Singh)
Decorative Use: High- and low-relief carvings, calligraphic inscriptions in marble and sandstone
Masonry Technique: Ashlar/coursed stone masonry with plaster decoration and glazed tiles
Weathering: Fortification wall collapsed in places; threatened by rainwater flooding, choking of original drainage system, and encroachment
Restoration: Minimal and discreet restoration avoiding modern materials; ongoing management under the Rohtas Fort Conservation Programme (initiated 2000)

Site Name: Archaeological Sites of Bat, Al-Khutm and Al-Ayn
Architecture Type: Settlements, Necropolises (tombs), Monumental Towers, Fort, Irrigation Systems/Dams
Construction Period: 3rd Millennium BCE (Bronze Age: Hafit Period, Early & Late Umm an-Nar periods; Iron Age; Neolithic)
Civilization: Early Bronze Age / Umm an-Nar & Hafit Cultures / Ancient Magan
Lithology: Dressed limestone blocks, Mud-brick (underneath earlier structures)
Quarry: Local ancient Bronze Age quarries & stone-masonry workshops
Local vs Imported: Local
Structural Use: Circular outer walls of towers, beehive tombs, cairn tombs, foundations, terraces
Masonry Technique: Dry-stone masonry, dressed blocks laid carefully with simple mud mortar
Weathering: Damaged slightly by action of time, natural erosion, and weathering processes
Restoration: Planned restoration for the monumental tower Kasr al-Rojoom

Architecture Type: Narrow, high, timber constructions with characteristic tall, slim clock towers at the western end (single- or double-roofed, covered by shingles)
Construction Period: Different periods / 1999 (UNESCO Inscription Year)
Civilization: Vernacular expression of the cultural landscape of northern Romania
Masonry Technique: Timber construction / Shingle roofing

Site Name: Historic Centre of Guimarães and Couros Zone
Architecture Type: Portuguese architecture (medieval settlement evolving into a modern town with grand houses, monastic complexes, tanneries, workers' houses)
Construction Period: 10th century CE, 12th century, 15th to 19th centuries (specifically 950–1498, 1498–1693)
Structural Use: Ground floor in granite with a half-timbered structure above
Masonry Technique: Granite combined with a timber-framed / half-timbered structure
Restoration: Rehabilitation strategy for the Couros Zone

Architecture Type: Complex series of platforms of dry-stone walled structures
Civilization: Torwa dynasty (arose from the collapse of the Great Zimbabwe Kingdom)
Local vs Imported: Local (Dry-stone building traditions surrounding the site area)
Structural Use: Retaining walls/revetments, platforms, passage ways, perambulatory galleries, Chief's residence (Mambo), terraces, and a monumental granite cross
Decorative Use: Retaining walls/revetments, platforms, passage ways, perambulatory galleries, Chief's residence (Mambo), terraces, and a monumental granite cross
Masonry Technique: Dry-stone masonry / Anastylosis (for restoration)
Weathering: Variations in temperature, rain-induced ground creepage (causing wall cracks, bulges, and collapses), ground water, natural erosion, encroaching vegetation
Replacement Stone: None used (restoration uses traditional methods/anastylosis with no new materials added)
Restoration: Anastylosis using traditional methods without adding new materials

Site Name: Qutb Minar and its Monuments, Delhi
Architecture Type: Architectural, Masonry, Monument, Mughal, Islamic, Funerary, Religious
Construction Period: Early 13th century (1199–1503; major expansions in 1311)
Civilization: Indo-Muslim / Islamic (Ghurid clans, Slave/Mamluk Dynasty, Khilji Dynasty, Mughal era influence)
Local vs Imported: Reused materials from ~20 local Brahman temples
Structural Use: Masonry tower, arches, minarets, arcades, ceremonial gateway, tomb chamber
Decorative Use: Carved inscriptional bands, arabesque patterns, geometrical patterns, carved pillars, cladding
Masonry Technique: Angular and rounded fluted masonry, ashlar masonry with carved bands
Restoration: Periodically undertaken reversible repairs respecting original construction systems

Site Name: Archaeological Park and Ruins of Quirigua
Architecture Type: Pyramids, terraces, staircases, dwellings, royal residence, administrative centre
Construction Period: 200 AD – 900 AD (Monuments carved 426 AD – 810 AD; major 8th-century remodelling)
Secondary Stone: Jade (mentioned in context of trade route control)
Structural Use: Pyramids, terraces, staircases, plazas
Decorative Use: Monolithic stelae, carved altars, sculpted calendars, zoomorphic & anthropomorphic sculptures
Masonry Technique: Carved in sandstone without the use of metal tools; high relief ("School of Motagua" style)
Weathering: Damage caused by environmental factors, flooding (1998, 2010), and proximity to Motagua River and geological fault
Restoration: Minimal conservation interventions; new shelter facilities and designs developed for individual monument protection

Construction Period: Built in 1532
Structural Use: Substructure, octagonal base, stairs, ciborium ("royal pew"), and pillars/walls
Decorative Use: Carved capitals, portals, and floor paving
Masonry Technique: Brickwork combined with white-stone carved features, cladding, and facing
Weathering: Complete loss of original white-stone carved capitals and portals on the second tier; surface damage to brickwork
Replacement Stone: New white-stone floors, stairs, and grounds; white limestone coatings for facades
Restoration: Major restorations occurred in the early 19th century, 1860s, early 20th century, 1980s, and 2003–2007

Architecture Type: Fortified city, temples/shrines with Iwans (open halls with high barrel-vaulted roofs), temenos, inner & outer circular defensive walls, towers, strongholds, gates, and bridges.
Construction Period: 1st century BC to mid-2nd century AD; Parthian empire era (ca. 200 BC–220 AD). Inscribed on UNESCO World Heritage list in 1985.
Civilization: Parthian Empire, Aramean, Arab Kingdom, Assyro-Babylonian with Hellenistic and Roman influences.
Quarry: Stone quarries near the site.
Local vs Imported: Local (relied for building materials on stone quarries near the site).
Structural Use: Inner main wall (diameter ~2 km), strongholds, towers, barrel-vaulted roofs / Iwans, rectangular temenos wall, contiguous Iwans, bridges, and wells.
Decorative Use: Carved stone and gypsum, pilastered portico, carved stone statues, statuettes of gods/rulers, sculptured figures, and Aramaic inscriptions.
Masonry Technique: Mudbrick, brick, bitumen and gypsum, stone and gypsum; barrel vaults; carved stone and chiselled stone masonry.
Weathering: Severe natural weathering, erosion, neglect, looting, and localized conflict-inflicted damage (2015–2017).
Replacement Stone: Stone cut and chiselled from the same local stone quarries by specialized archaeologists and Mosul stone chisellers.
Restoration: Historical conservation/restoration of walls and temenos rooms; ongoing plans for archaeological conservation of natural/conflict erosion.

Architecture Type: Walled trading town, ramparts, warehouses, merchants' dwellings, castles, cathedrals, churches, guild houses, residences, towers, gates, moats, monasteries, vernacular wooden houses, hospital, school, prison.
Construction Period: Viking Age (pre-12th century), 12th century, 13th century, 14th century, 18th century, and 19th century.
Civilization: Viking / Gotlandic, German (Hanseatic League), Danish, Russian.
Quarry: Quarries extracted from the surrounding dry moats, open spaces, and adjacent limestone quarries.
Local vs Imported: Local
Transport Distance: Local / Short distance (Quarried directly from the defensive network/moats surrounding the site and nearby quarries).
Structural Use: Town defense walls, ramparts, towers, gates, foundations, warehouses, churches, cathedrals, and street paving (large limestone slabs).
Masonry Technique: Stone masonry with Gotlandic lime plaster/mortars; horizontal plank timber construction (used for vernacular houses).
Weathering: Partial collapses over the years (e.g., collapse of a section of the town wall in 2012); decay of abandoned church ruins after the Reformation (1530s).
Restoration: Restoration of the town wall following the 2012 collapse; ongoing traditional lime production and craftsmanship for mortar/plaster preservation.

Construction Period: 13th century onwards (severely damaged by earthquake in 1667; damaged in 1990s by armed conflict)
Structural Use: Bridge, Capital, Castle, Cathedral, Church, Minaret, Monastery, Mosque, Palace, Sanctuary, Temple, Tomb, Tower
Restoration: Focus of a major restoration programme co-ordinated by UNESCO

Site Name: St Mary's Cathedral and St Michael's Church at Hildesheim
Kutná Hora: Historical Town Centre with the Church of St Barbara and the Cathedral of Our Lady at Sedlec

Architecture Type: Cistercian abbey / Abbey, Monastery complex (includes church, cloister, fortified royal residence, chapter house, refectory, pantheon, etc.)
Construction Period: 12th to 15th centuries (Church dates to the 13th century)
Civilization: Crown of Aragon / Kingdom of Catalonia and Aragon
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Alabaster (retable of the high altar by Damià Forment, 1529)
Decorative Use: High altar retable (made of alabaster)
Restoration: Retable of the high altar, church, refectory, cloister, chapter house, scriptorium, and abbot’s palace restored since 1940

Site Name: La Lonja de la Seda de Valencia
Construction Period: 1482–1533 (Built from 1483; 15th and 16th centuries)
Quarry: Alcublas
Local vs Imported: Local / Regional
Structural Use: Twisted columns, helical pillars, cross vaults, walls, tower, all in freestone
Decorative Use: Paved floor (Alcublas marble), grotesque gargoyles, carved decoration, richly decorated sills and lintels, portrait medallions
Masonry Technique: Freestone ashlar / helical masonry
Restoration: Stucco decoration added in 1832; repair of roofs in 1891–1920; raising of the tower with crenelations reproducing original style; recent meticulous restoration

Site Name: Old Town of Ávila with its Extra-Muros Churches
Harar Jugol, the Fortified Historic Town
Selimiye Mosque and its Social Complex
Architecture Type: Mosque complex (Ottoman külliye comprising mosque, madrasas, covered market, clock house, outer courtyard, library)
Construction Period: 16th Century (built between 1568 and 1575)
Structural Use: Dome, four minarets, madrasas, covered market, clock house, library, courtyard
Decorative Use: Iznik ceramic tiles

Site Name: Studley Royal Park including the Ruins of Fountains Abbey
Country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland

Site Name: Historic Centre of Mexico City and Xochimilco
City of Luxembourg: its Old Quarters and Fortifications
Sanctuary of Bom Jesus do Congonhas
Architecture Type: Sanctuary, Basilica, Church, Chapels, Outdoor staircase
Construction Period: Second half of the 18th century (Church completed in 1772)
Decorative Use: Parapets of the divided staircase, statues of the 12 prophets, and general stonework
Restoration: Conservation and reclamation programs supported by IPHAN and the Monumenta Program

Site Name: Roman Monuments, Cathedral of St Peter and Church of Our Lady in Trier
Architecture Type: Roman, Romanesque, Gothic, High Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque
Civilization: Roman / Western Roman Empire / German Christian Medieval
Structural Use: Fortified gate, city walls, basilicas, thermae/baths, bridge, column, cathedral, church structures
Decorative Use: Baroque marble altars, archbishops' tombs, painted ceiling
Masonry Technique: Large stone construction (Porta Nigra), brick monumental structures (Basilica)
Restoration: Reconstructed/restored in the 19th century; WW2 damage restored between 1946–1949 (Church of Our Lady) and 1954–1956 (Basilica)

Architecture Type: Earthen architecture, domestic, monumental, religious, and civil buildings
Construction Period: Founded 1527; surviving structures primarily date from the 17th century
Civilization: Spanish colonial with local indigenous and Dutch influences
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Adobe (sun-dried mud brick), Bahareque (mud, timber, bamboo), Tapia (rammed earth)
Quarry: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Structural Use: Earthen structures / Mud-building systems (adobe, bahareque, tapia) for walls and foundations
Masonry Technique: Bahareque (mud, timber, bamboo), Adobe (sun-dried brick), Tapia (rammed earth)
Weathering: Damage from heavy rains, climatic change, and material fragility
Restoration: Emergency interventions, PLINCODE conservation plan, earthen technique restoration

Architecture Type: Homogeneous architectural ensemble; multi-storeyed tower-houses, minarets, mosques, hammams (baths), caravanserais, and city walls.
Construction Period: Houses and structures built continuously over 2,500 years; pre-11th century houses and mosques; 16th-century Ottoman additions.
Civilization: Pre-Islamic Yemenite kingdoms, Abyssinian (Christian, 525–575 AD), Islamic / Arab, and Ottoman civilizations.
Colour: White (gypsum); Ochre / Bistre (earth and burnt brick)
Local vs Imported: Local materials ("exceptional craftsmanship in the use of local materials and techniques")
Transport Distance: Nearby/Immediate local area ("bistre-colored earth of the nearby mountains")
Structural Use: Stone-built ground floors supporting upper multi-storeyed tower-houses; city boundary walls.
Decorative Use: Fired brick combined with white gypsum applied for geometric surface patterns on building facades.
Masonry Technique: Stone masonry for lower levels; rammed earth (pisé) and burnt brick for upper floors.
Restoration: Traditional conservation practices noted; vulnerable to incorrect contemporary conservation practices and development.

Site Name: 18th-Century Royal Palace at Caserta with the Park, the Aqueduct of Vanvitelli, and the San Leucio Complex
Cathedral and Churches of Echmiatsin and the Archaeological Site of Zvartnots
Architecture Type: Central-domed cross-hall type church, four-column domed basilica, three-aisled basilica, circular structure
Construction Period: AD 301–303 (Cathedral), AD 618 (St. Hripsime), AD 630 (St. Gayane), Middle of 7th century (Zvartnots), AD 1694 (St. Shoghakat), AD 1767 (St. Mariam Astvatsatsin)
Structural Use: Walls, dome/roofing slabs, pillars, vaults, foundations, columns
Decorative Use: Internal marble painting surface (main apse fresco base), rich bas-reliefs and high-reliefs
Masonry Technique: Well-processed tuff blocks
Replacement Stone: Lead roofing replaced by stone slabs (2000 AD)
Restoration: Roof and walls renovated in 1652; monastery renovated 1866–1882; dome stone slabs added in 2000

Architecture Type: Mughal Architecture / Indo-Islamic Style (Palaces, Public Buildings, Mosques, Living Areas, Fortifications)
Construction Period: 1571–1573 (City construction); Mosque completed in 1571–72; Tomb completed in 1580–81 / 1606; Buland Darwaza completed in 1575
UNESCO Criteria: (ii) to exhibit an important interchange of human values, over a span of time or within a cultural area of the world, on developments in architecture or technology, monumental arts, town-planning or landscape design; | (iii) to bear a unique or at least exceptional testimony to a cultural tradition or to a civilization which is living or which has disappeared; | (iv) to be an outstanding example of a type of building, architectural or technological ensemble or landscape which illustrates (a) significant stage(s) in human history;
Geological Age: Vindhyan (Stratigraphic Group mentioned for hill ranges)
Quarry: Outcrops of the Vindhyan hill ranges (Mining within 10-km radius banned)

Structural Use: Wall, Porticos, Pillars, Roofs, Gate (Buland Darwaza), Towers (Hiran Minar), Fortification
Decorative Use: Perforated stone screens, Sculpted decoration, Richly carved interior pillars, Azure-blue ribbed roof
Masonry Technique: Perforated stone screens, Sculpted decoration, Richly carved interior pillars, Azure-blue ribbed roof
Restoration: Repairs, chemical preservation, and restoration carried out on Buland Darwaza, Jama Masjid, Shaikh Salim Chisti's Tomb, Khwabgah, Mariam's house, Jodha Bai Palace, Diwan-i-Am, etc.

Site Name: Archaeological Areas of Pompei, Herculaneum and Torre Annunziata
Architecture Type: Residential, Commercial, and Public Urban Architecture (Atrium Houses, Suburban Villas, Forum, Baths, Theatres, Amphitheatre, Palaestra, Thermopolia/Shops)
Construction Period: Early Roman Empire / 3rd century BC to 1st century AD (79 AD Eruption)
UNESCO Criteria: (iii) to bear a unique or at least exceptional testimony to a cultural tradition or to a civilization which is living or which has disappeared; | (iv) to be an outstanding example of a type of building, architectural or technological ensemble or landscape which illustrates (a) significant stage(s) in human history; | (v) to be an outstanding example of a traditional human settlement, land-use, or sea-use which is representative of a culture (or cultures), or human interaction with the environment especially when it has become vulnerable under the impact of irreversible change;
Lithology: Volcanic Ash Deposit and Pyroclastic Lapilli
Local vs Imported: Local (Volcanic eruptive deposits originating from Mount Vesuvius)
Structural Use: Enclosure and load-bearing walls, colonnades, paving, and urban infrastructure
Decorative Use: Wall paintings, frescos, illusionistic architectural motifs, and mosaic floors
Weathering: Active environmental decay, collapse risks from water/exposure, and historical thermal carbonization during the eruption
Restoration: Modern consolidation, progressive introduction of enduring techniques/materials, 1950s open-air museum restoration (Amedeo Maiuri), and structural stabilization under the "Grande Progetto Pompei" (2012)

Construction Period: Late 15th century to end of 19th century
UNESCO Criteria: (ii) to exhibit an important interchange of human values, over a span of time or within a cultural area of the world, on developments in architecture or technology, monumental arts, town-planning or landscape design; | (iv) to be an outstanding example of a type of building, architectural or technological ensemble or landscape which illustrates (a) significant stage(s) in human history;
Quarry: Castle wall and tower masonry construction
Restoration: Restored at the end of the 19th century after suffering severe damage during the Napoleonic period
